_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for DeWitt County, IL. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses that shape what genealogists can and cannot find.

current_courthouse.notes: The 1913 Neoclassical courthouse sits on the Clinton public square. Clinton is an Illinois Central Railroad town — the railroad reached Clinton in 1854 and transformed the county's economy.

previous_courthouses.location: Clinton, original log and frame courthouse
previous_courthouses.fate: DeWitt County was formed from Macon and McLean counties in 1839. The first courthouse was a modest structure. Records transferred to the successor building.

previous_courthouses.location: Clinton, brick courthouse on the public square
previous_courthouses.fate: Abraham Lincoln tried cases in this courthouse while riding the Eighth Judicial Circuit. Replaced due to growth.
Served during the railroad boom era. Replaced by the current 1913 building. Records survived all transitions.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or catastrophic loss
record_losses.description: DeWitt County has experienced no major courthouse fire or catastrophic record loss. Records survive continuously from the county's 1839 formation. The county seat of Clinton was a stop on Abraham Lincoln's Eighth Judicial Circuit, and court records from that era are intact. The Illinois Central Railroad's arrival in 1854 generated a surge of land transactions and business records that are fully preserved.
record_losses.narrative_impact: DeWitt County's complete record survival means that if {ancestor_name} bought land, married, or had an estate probated here after 1839, the paper trail exists. Lincoln practiced law in this courthouse, and the same case files that document his practice may document {ancestor_name}'s neighbors, clients, or adversaries.
record_losses.source: History of DeWitt County, Illinois. Chicago: Pioneer Publishing, 1910.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: No known gaps. The Illinois Central Railroad land grants (1850s) generated a wave of land sales that are fully documented. Pre-1839 deeds for the future DeWitt County are filed in Macon or McLean county records.
deeds.repository: DeWitt County Recorder, Clinton; Illinois State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com IL land records

wills_estates.gaps: Early estate records are sparse, reflecting the small pioneer population.
wills_estates.repository: DeWitt County Probate Court, Clinton; Illinois State Archives; IRAD at UIS

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage licenses from county formation
marriages.gaps: Earliest records are minimal in detail. Coverage improves after 1850.
marriages.repository: DeWitt County Clerk, Clinton; Illinois State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good to excellent — circuit court minutes from formation. These include cases from Abraham Lincoln's Eighth Judicial Circuit rides.
court_minutes.gaps: Justice of the peace records are incomplete. Circuit court records are intact.
court_minutes.repository: DeWitt County Circuit Court, Clinton; Illinois State Archives; Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library
court_minutes.online_access: Lincoln Legal Papers project (digital); Illinois State Archives microfilm

vital_records.completeness: Illinois required county-level registration from 1877. Compliance was inconsistent until statewide enforcement in 1916. Clinton town records may be more complete than rural township records.
vital_records.repository: DeWitt County Clerk; Illinois Department of Public Health; Illinois State Archives

substitute_sources.description: The Illinois Central reached Clinton in 1854, making DeWitt County a railroad hub. Railroad land sale records, station records, and employment files document both land purchasers and railroad workers.
substitute_sources.repository: Newberry Library, Chicago (Illinois Central Railroad archives); Illinois State Archives; DeWitt County Museum Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Illinois Central land sale records list purchasers by name, parcel, price, and date — providing an alternative to county deed records for families who bought railroad land. Railroad employment records, where they survive, document workers by name, occupation, and date of hire. The Newberry Library's Illinois Central collection is the most comprehensive repository.
substitute_sources.source: Stover, John F. History of the Illinois Central Railroad. New York: Macmillan, 1975.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1840–1940 (first full census after formation). Agricultural schedules (1850–1880) and manufacturing schedules document the county's transition from subsistence farming to railroad-connected commercial agriculture.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The 1860 census is particularly valuable for DeWitt County because it captures the population after the railroad's arrival — revealing new settlers drawn by the Illinois Central. Agricultural schedules show the shift from subsistence to surplus farming once railroad access made shipping profitable.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, DeWitt County, IL.

substitute_sources.source_type: Macon and McLean County records (pre-1839 parent counties)
substitute_sources.description: DeWitt County was formed from Macon and McLean counties in 1839. Families who settled before formation appear in the parent county records.
substitute_sources.repository: Macon County Recorder, Decatur; McLean County Recorder, Bloomington; Illinois State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracing pioneer families before 1839. The researcher must determine which parent county's territory included the ancestor's land to search the correct records.
substitute_sources.source: History of DeWitt County, Illinois. Chicago: Pioneer Publishing, 1910.

narrative_hooks: DeWitt County's records run unbroken from 1839, and Lincoln rode the Eighth Judicial Circuit through Clinton — if {ancestor_name} appeared in this county's circuit court before 1860, the case may be indexed in the Lincoln Legal Papers project.

The Illinois Central Railroad reached Clinton in 1854, transforming DeWitt County from a frontier backwater into a shipping hub — if {ancestor_name} arrived after that date, the railroad may have been the reason, and the Newberry Library's Illinois Central records may document the land purchase.

Before 1839, what became DeWitt County was split between Macon and McLean counties — if {ancestor_name} was among the earliest settlers, {pronoun_possessive} records are filed in Decatur or Bloomington, not Clinton.
Vital registration in DeWitt County was unreliable before 1916 — the absence of a birth or death record for {ancestor_name} reflects the system's failures, not the family's existence.

If {ancestor_name} bought Illinois Central Railroad land in DeWitt County in the 1850s or 1860s, the railroad's own land sale records at the Newberry Library may preserve details — parcel, price, terms — that supplement or even predate the county deed recording.
source_references: History of DeWitt County, Illinois. Chicago: Pioneer Publishing, 1910.
Stover, John F. History of the Illinois Central Railroad. New York: Macmillan, 1975.
Illinois State Archives. Descriptive Inventory of the Archives of the State of Illinois. 2nd ed. Springfield: Office of the Secretary of State, 1978.
National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, DeWitt County, IL.
